nodes.label: The convex readout — why the 20% is the easy part
nodes.rationale: Answers the instinct to fear the GD side: fixing the bulk and training only a linear readout turns credit assignment into a single convex problem, no bad local minima — but with two honest boundaries (convex is the regression not the deployed cost; we don't inherit the reservoir's random-projection universal-approximation guarantee).

nodes.label: Reservoir computing / ELM (frozen bulk + trained readout)
nodes.rationale: Echo-state networks / liquid state machines / ELM: take a large fixed nonlinear system, don't train it, train only a linear readout — exactly the draft-6.0 pattern in the readout sense, but the bulk is unsupervised-trained then frozen, not random, so it's reservoir-like but not a reservoir proper (doesn't inherit free device-mismatch tolerance, which is why A7/noise is a real open risk).

nodes.label: Online Convex Optimization / FTRL (the right lens for streaming)
nodes.rationale: The readout sees a stream of (feature,label) pairs and must keep low regret vs the best fixed readout in hindsight; the comparator moves (SCFF drifts), so this is OCO with a non-stationary comparator whose regret scales with drift/path-length, not sqrt(T) — tracking the drift, not solving the regression, is the real cost.

nodes.label: 'The 20% is the easy part' — with the deployment-cost caveat
nodes.rationale: Convex for a linear head; SGD+little momentum is enough, Adam/Lion are conveniences not necessities; RanPAC's running-mean+Gram closed-form is the most substrate-native readout provided the ridge solve is affordable — but the deployed-readout substrate cost is Phase-7/8's meter to still answer, and a 2-3 layer MLP head or multimodal mixture reopens non-convexity.
